fuzzy_prd:
We need a set of file-inspection and code-understanding helpers for our coding assistant. Right now different parts of the tool re-implement language detection, query parsing, and file editing in slightly incompatible ways. We want one shared toolkit with a clean contract. Six operations are needed: first, map a file's extension to a programming language name — the assistant needs this to decide how to handle each file without reading its contents; second, extract likely code identifiers from a natural-language question — only meaningful tokens, filtering out short words and common programming stop-words; third, turn a question into a list of file glob patterns worth searching — language-family patterns based on trigger words found in the question, plus some sensible defaults; fourth, produce a quick structural outline of a source file using a line-by-line scan — function names, class names, structs, etc., up to thirty items; fifth, render a file's content with line numbers, with optional windowed slicing by offset and limit; sixth, do a safe in-place text replacement that only proceeds when the target string appears exactly once. The unique-edit feature should handle ambiguity the same way our config-file patcher handles it — if the target appears multiple times or not at all, report a specific error with the count, never silently corrupt the content. Line number rendering uses a four-character field, right-justified.

A couple quick clarifications from the team chat so nobody has to guess. For the unique edit behavior, the error format should stay very literal. If the target shows up more than once, return exactly two lines: error=multiple_occurrences\ncount=<n> where n is the exact number of occurrences. If it is not found, return exactly error=string_not_found as a single line with no count. That keeps it aligned with the existing patcher behavior and makes the failure mode easy to reason about.

On identifier extraction, the stop-word filtering is case-insensitive, and the stop words are exactly this, that, from, what, when, where, which, find, function, class, struct, impl, type, interface, const, static, public, private, protected, export, import. Tokens also need to be >3 chars, alphanumeric+underscore only, and not purely numeric. So the idea is still to keep only the likely useful code-ish terms from the question, not filler.

For the search glob generation, if no '**/src/...' pattern was produced, append these four defaults: **/src/**/*.rs, **/src/**/*.ts, **/src/**/*.js, **/src/**/*.py. Also, **/*.rs is always guaranteed to be present at the end if not already there. That last bit is intentional even when other patterns already exist, since Rust files are common enough in the repos this assistant sees.

trigger_keywords: what is the line number format for read_lines
number field width in read_lines output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Line numbers are right-justified in a 4-character field, followed by ' | ' (space pipe space), then the line text. No trailing newline after the final line. Example: '   1 | content'

trigger_keywords: what is the outline header format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: First output line: 'file=<basename> language=<language> lines=<total_line_count>'. Then each construct: '<kind>\t<name>\t<line_number>' (TAB-separated, 1-based line numbers). Max 30 constructs.

trigger_keywords: what happens when java and javascript are both present
java trigger when javascript is present
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: java patterns are added only when 'javascript' is NOT present in the lowercased query. If 'javascript' appears, the java family patterns are suppressed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same way our config-file patcher handles it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The unique-edit operation in the toolkit: it reports error=string_not_found when the target is missing, or error=multiple_occurrences\ncount=<n> when found more than once.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_detect_language.json.description: Determine the programming language associated with each supplied file path purely from its file-name extension. The handler receives a list of path strings and emits, for every path in order, one line pairing the original path with the detected language name (or a neutral 'unknown' marker when the extension is not recognised or absent). Extension matching is case-insensitive and independent of any directory components in the path.

cases.expected_output:
src/main.rs	rust
components/Button.jsx	javascript
app.ts	typescript
class.cpp	cpp
header.h	c
main.go	go
Main.java	java
test.py	python
unknown.xyz	unknown
test	unknown

feature2_extract_terms.json.description: Extract candidate code identifiers from a free-form natural-language query so they can later be used as search terms. The query is split on whitespace; only whitespace tokens longer than three characters are considered, each is trimmed of surrounding non-identifier punctuation, and a token is kept only if the trimmed result is longer than three characters, consists solely of letters/digits/underscores, is not purely numeric, and is not one of a fixed set of common English/programming stop-words (compared case-insensitively). Surviving identifiers are emitted one per line preserving their order of appearance; a query yielding no identifiers produces empty output.

input.query: Show me how CodeParser uses tree_sitter
How do I use this function?

feature3_relevant_files.json.description: Derive a list of candidate file-glob patterns for a codebase query. Quoted file names referenced in the query (single or double quoted, of the form name.ext) become explicit '**/<name.ext>' patterns. Language-family patterns are then appended whenever the lower-cased query contains the corresponding trigger substrings (e.g. rust/.rs, javascript/js/node/react, typescript/ts/angular/next, python/py/django/flask, go/golang, c++/cpp/' c '/.c, java but not javascript). If no source-directory ('**/src/...') pattern has been added, four default source-directory patterns are appended; finally a '**/*.rs' pattern is guaranteed to be present. Patterns are emitted one per line in the order produced, including any duplicates.

input.query: Analyze the Rust code in this project

feature4_outline.json.description: Produce a lightweight structural outline of a source file using a line-oriented scan (no full parser). Input supplies a file name, a language label, and the raw source text. The first output line is a file header reporting the file's base name, its language label, and its total line count. Each subsequent line describes one detected top-level construct in source order: its kind (function, class, struct, trait, impl, interface, type), an extracted name, and its 1-based line number. Constructs are recognised by keyword patterns appearing on a line (e.g. 'fn', 'struct', 'impl', 'trait', 'mod'/'def'/'func'/'class'/'function'/'async'); the name is the token following the recognised keyword with trailing punctuation stripped. At most thirty constructs are reported.

cases.expected_output:
file=test.rs language=rust lines=32
struct	TestStruct	2
impl	TestStruct	7
function	new	8
function	test_method(&self	15
function	test_function	20
trait	TestTrait	24
function	trait_method(&self	25
function	module_function	29

feature5_1_number_all_lines.json.description: Render the full text content of a file annotated with line numbers. Every line of the content is emitted as a 1-based line number right-justified in a four-character field, followed by ' | ', followed by the original line text. Lines are joined by newlines with no trailing newline after the final line.

input.content:
This is a test file
With multiple lines
For testing file operations

cases.expected_output:
1 | This is a test file
   2 | With multiple lines
   3 | For testing file operations

feature5_2_windowed_lines.json.description: Render a windowed slice of a file's lines with line numbers. Input supplies the content, a zero-based starting offset, and an optional limit (omitted means 'to end of file'). The starting offset is clamped to the number of available lines; the end is the offset plus limit (or end of file), also clamped. Each selected line is emitted as its original 1-based line number right-justified in a four-character field, then ' | ', then the line text, joined by newlines with no trailing newline. An offset at or beyond the line count, or a zero limit, yields empty output.

feature6_unique_edit.json.description: Perform an exact-match, single-occurrence in-place string replacement within a file's content. Input supplies the original content plus an 'old' substring and its 'new' replacement. If the old substring occurs exactly once, it is replaced and the full resulting content is emitted. If it does not occur at all, a neutral error line 'error=string_not_found' is emitted. If it occurs more than once, the replacement is refused and a neutral two-line error 'error=multiple_occurrences' plus 'count=<n>' (the number of occurrences) is emitted, so an ambiguous edit fails fast instead of corrupting the file.

input.content:
This is the original content
That needs to be modified
For testing purposes

cases.expected_output:
This is the modified content
That needs to be modified
For testing purposes

input.content:
This is a test file
For testing edit operations
